articles.title: Punk Rocker Egrets Steal the Show at Smithsonian Photo Contest
articles.summary: Three baby egrets with spiky feathers that look exactly like punk rocker mohawks have won the grand prize in the Smithsonian Magazine's 23rd annual photo contest. Photographer Jacqueline Burke, a 64-year-old veterinarian, captured the chicks squabbling at a New Jersey rookery. Over 17,000 submissions from 108 countries were entered. Other winners include a barefoot devotee at a Hindu festival, a Navajo teen on her horse during a coming-of-age ceremony, and a rare chameleon. The contest celebrates the beauty of nature, culture, and architecture. The winning images appear in the April/May 2026 issue of Smithsonian magazine.

articles.title: Feathered Neighbors: Peacocks Divide a California Town
articles.summary: In Arcadia, California, a town 45 minutes from downtown Los Angeles, dozens of peacocks roam freely—strutting on rooftops, pecking at cars, and displaying their brilliant feathers. But not everyone loves them. Introduced over a century ago by businessman Elias Lucky Baldwin, the birds are protected by local laws, leading to an overpopulation problem. Filmmaker Callie Barlow's short documentary "Our Neighbors, the Peacocks" explores the deep divide between residents who adore the birds and those who want them relocated. The film resists offering a simple solution, instead suggesting that living alongside these animals requires "putting aside your own discomforts to find a deeper meaning in nature."

articles.title: Venice Biennale Jury Excludes Russia and Israel from Awards Over ICC Charges
articles.summary: The all-women jury for the 61st Venice Biennale has announced it will not consider countries whose leaders are charged with crimes against humanity by the International Criminal Court for awards. This effectively excludes Russia and Israel, whose presidents Vladimir Putin and Benjamin Netanyahu face ICC arrest warrants. The jury's statement comes amid mounting criticism of the Biennale Foundation for allowing both nations to participate. Nearly 10,000 people signed a letter opposing Russia's return, and the European Union threatened to suspend a €2 million grant. Artists in the central exhibition support the jury's decision, though some activists say the move doesn't go far enough.
